en.extract:
The Louisiade pitta (Erythropitta meeki) is a species of the pitta. It was considered a subspecies of the red-bellied pitta. It is endemic to Rossel Island in the Louisiade Archipelago in Papua New Guinea. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests. It may be threatened by habitat loss, but is currently listed by the IUCN as Data Deficient as there have been no definite records since the type specimen was collected in 1898. However an expedition to the island in 2014 revealed that local people said they still encountered it. In 2022, British tourist Michael Smith found two alive individuals with clear photograph evidence.

The '''Louisiade pitta''' ('''''Erythropitta meeki''''') is a species of the [[pitta]]. It was considered a subspecies of the [[red-bellied pitta]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Rossel Island]] in the [[Louisiade Archipelago]] in [[Papua New Guinea]]. Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s. It may be threatened by [[habitat loss]], but is currently listed by the [[IUCN]] as Data Deficient as there have been no definite records since the type specimen was collected in 1898. However an expedition to the island in 2014 revealed that local people said they still encountered it.<ref name="iucn status 19 November 2021">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2020 |title=''Erythropitta meeki'' |volume=2020 |article-number=e.T103656460A180755077 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-3.RLTS.T103656460A180755077.en |access-date=19 November 2021}}</ref> In 2022, British tourist Michael Smith found two alive individuals with clear photograph evidence.<ref>{{cite web|author=Smith, M.|date=January 10, 2024|title=I discovered a tree kangaroo that had only been seen once – by the man who shot it in 1928|website=The Guardian|url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2024/jan/10/i-discovered-tree-kangaroo-only-seen-once-1928-aoe|access-date=April 22, 2024}}</ref>
